about_subtitle: A city that once built America—and deserves honest leadership again.
about_hope_quote: We are a hopeful people for the future, but we have to get rid of the corruption.
about_past_p1: For generations, Bridgeport wasn't just another Connecticut city. It was a place where things got built. P.T. Barnum helped turn its east side into a manufacturing quarter. Immigrants from Ireland, Italy, Hungary, and around the world came to work the brass mills, sewing-machine factories, and cartridge plants along the Pequonnock.
about_past_p2: By the 1930s, more than 500 factories operated here. During two world wars, Bridgeport was called an arsenal of democracy—Remington's plant on Boston Avenue was once among the largest factories in America. At mid-century, nearly 159,000 people lived here. Lincoln campaigned in its City Hall. Olmsted designed its parks. The Frisbie Pie Company gave the world the flying disc. This was a proud, working city—and everyone knew it.
about_decline_p1: Then the mills left. After World War II, manufacturing moved south and overseas. Jobs vanished. Families followed work to the suburbs. Population fell decade after decade. Remington closed in 1989. In 1991, Bridgeport filed for bankruptcy—the largest municipal bankruptcy in American history at that time.
about_decline_p2: Whole neighborhoods emptied out. Factories sat dark. While nearby Stamford became a corporate hub, Bridgeport stayed locked in an industrial past with a shrinking tax base, rising poverty, and decades of disinvestment. The city that had built so much for the country was told to wait its turn.
about_corruption_p1: Bridgeport didn't just lose industry—it lost honest government. In the 1990s and 2000s, federal prosecutors convicted Mayor Joe Ganim on racketeering, extortion, and bribery for shaking down developers and steering city contracts. He went to prison for seven years. Then Bridgeport elected him mayor again.
about_corruption_p2: Contracts still went to insiders. Promises piled up. City officials were convicted. Too little changed on the street. Corruption didn't cause every factory to close—but it poisoned the recovery, rewarded the wrong people, and taught a generation not to trust City Hall.
about_hope_p1: But Bridgeport is not finished. The same pride that built this city—immigrant grit, union halls, church basements, block parties on the East Side and in Black Rock—still lives here. We are a hopeful people for the future. We believe in this place. We believe our kids deserve safe streets, good schools, and leadership that answers to neighbors.
about_hope_p2: Hope is not a slogan. Before Bridgeport can rise again, we have to get rid of the corruption. We have to stop rewarding insiders. We have to build a government that works for residents, not for deals made behind closed doors. That's what Build Back Bridgeport is about—not nostalgia, but a return to what this city was at its best, and a break from what's held it back.
about_compact_p1: Bridgeport was once a manufacturing powerhouse—an arsenal of democracy during two world wars, home to hundreds of factories, and nearly 159,000 residents at its peak. Immigrants built lives here. Lincoln spoke here. This city made things the world needed.
about_compact_p2: Then industry left. Jobs disappeared. Remington closed. In 1991, the city filed for the largest municipal bankruptcy in American history. Meanwhile, corruption at City Hall—from kickbacks and racketeering to mayors convicted and re-elected—taught residents not to trust the government that was supposed to help them recover.
about_compact_p3: We are a hopeful people for the future, but we have to get rid of the corruption. The pride that built Bridgeport is still here. Build Back Bridgeport exists to honor that history, confront what's broken, and fight for honest leadership so this city can belong to its residents again.
about_image_decline_alt: Bridgeport after decades of industrial decline
about_image_hope_alt: Bridgeport residents looking toward the future
volunteer_subtitle: Every person makes a difference. Choose how you'd like to help build Bridgeport back.
volunteer_image_alt: Build Back Bridgeport yard sign in a neighborhood
volunteer_option_share_desc: Spread the word to neighbors, family, and coworkers. Share our website and social posts so more residents learn about rebuilding Bridgeport.
volunteer_option_canvass_desc: Help us reach residents face to face. Put up flyers in your neighborhood, knock doors, and talk with neighbors about our plans.
volunteer_option_leader_desc: Coordinate volunteer efforts in your part of the city. Neighborhood leaders organize canvassing, events, and stay in touch with the campaign team.
contact_response_note: Have a question about our plans, interested in volunteering, or want to share feedback on rebuilding Bridgeport? We'd love to hear from you. Reach out by phone or email and someone from our team will follow up. Please allow 1–3 business days for a reply, especially when we're out in the community or during busy campaign periods—but we aim to respond within 24 hours whenever possible. Your message matters to us, and we appreciate your patience as we work to answer every inquiry thoughtfully.
